data.title: GOP rep: 'Why should you ban ICE from being at polling places?'
data.description:
Rep. Jason Smith (R-Mo.) on Thursday questioned why Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) would be banned from polling places ahead of midterm elections. 

“Why should you ban ICE from being at polling places? Because illegals aren’t supposed to vote in this America,” Smith said during an appearance on CNBC’s “Squawk Box.”

“I mean, like, it does not make sense, the requests that they’re having, enforce the rule of law, and that’s what President Trump is doing. Apparently Democrats don’t like the rule of law. If they don’t like the rule of law, they need to change it,” he added.

In recent weeks, Democrats have raised concerns with ICE and other immigration authorities who wear masks, operate without judicial warrants and question their use of force while interacting with the general public. 

Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) said ICE agents could try to “intimidate” voters who are heading to polling places to cast their ballot following the death of two U.S. citizens in Minnesota.

“I am, we are all, greatly afraid with these roving ICE vans that we see in Minneapolis and other cities. Could those ICE roving vans be used to try to go and intimidate voters at the polling station?” Warner asked during a conversation about nationalizing the federal election. 

“These are all things that, you know, sounded maybe way out or draconian a year ago or a year and a half ago, but they are at our front door right now,” he continued. 

However, Smith suggested the presence of immigration authorities would not ward off U.S. citizens from voting.

“They have never changed it, but they’re complaining now,” Smith said in regard to the law that bans undocumented immigrants from voting in federal elections. 

On Tuesday, former White House strategist Steve Bannon said that ICE would “surround the polls come November” during his “War Room” podcast.

“We’re not going to sit here and allow you to steal the country again. And you can whine and cry and throw your toys out of the pram all you want, but we will never again allow an election to be stolen,” he continued.

There is little evidence that non-citizens have voted substantially in federal elections, but the topic has become a recent focus for the Trump administration amid the push to crack down on illegal immigration.

data.title: Speaker Johnson: Not 'appropriate' for DOJ to track members Epstein files searches
data.description:
Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said Thursday that it is not “appropriate” for the Department of Justice (DOJ) to track the searches of members of Congress viewing unredacted Jeffrey Epstein files, adding that he guessed any such surveillance was an “oversight.”

“My understanding is that there are computers set up where the DOJ was allowing access to the files. And I think members should obviously have the right to peruse those at their own speed and with their own discretion,” Johnson told reporters.

“I don’t think it’s appropriate for anybody to be tracking that,” Johnson said. “So, I will echo that to anybody involved with the DOJ. And I’m sure it was an oversight. That’s my guess, okay?”

The statement comes as lawmakers have balked at the DOJ seemingly tracking the activity of members of Congress who go to its to see unredacted versions of the files related to the late sex offender.

Attorney General Pam Bondi was apparently prepared with background on such searches for her testimony before the House Judiciary Committee on Wednesday. Photographers in the room captured photos of Bondi’s binder of notes during the hearing that included a list of items that Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.) searched in the files.

““It is an outrage that DOJ is tracking Members’ investigative steps undertaken to ensure that DOJ is complying with the Epstein File Transparency Act and using this information for the Attorney General’s embarrassing polemical purposes,” House Judiciary Committee ranking member Jamie Raskin (D-Md.) said in a statement, calling on the department to immediately cease tracking lawmaker activity. Raskin added that he will be “asking the DOJ Inspector General to open an inquiry into this outrageous abuse of power.”

Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), one of the four Republicans who joined with Democrats to force a vote on releasing the files, said on social media she could confirm the DOJ is tracking lawmakers’ activity in the files. She told reporters of the monitoring: “It’s creepy.”

House Minority Leader Hakkeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) said of the tracking in a press conference: “It’s a disgrace. It does violate the principles of separate and coequal branches of government.”

data.title: Hawley, Minnesota AG get into shouting match during Senate hearing
data.description:
Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) and Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison got into a shouting match during a Senate hearing on Thursday morning over a federal investigation into fraud in the state. 

The senator accused Ellison of being complicit in a fraud that resulted in the theft of federal funds allocated for Minnesota social service programs. Federal prosecutors estimate that millions of dollars were stolen during this scheme.

“You’ve been right at the center of this project from the beginning, and you’ve enabled it, and you should resign,” Hawley said during his turn to ask questions at the Senate Homeland Security Committee hearing.. 

Ellison retorted, “You should resign. I was thinking the same thing about you.”

Hawley, with his voice rising, lobbed a series of accusations at Ellison and suggested that the attorney general should be “indicted” as Ellison vehemently denied the lawmakers’ claims. 

Hawley claimed that Ellison and his family profited from the scheme. A Minnesota whistleblower has alleged that Ellison and other state officials knew about the fraud scheme as early as 2019, but that the officials failed to intervene. 

“For the record, he’s lying, and that’s the truth,” Ellison said. 

The interaction was tense from the beginning, with Ellison refusing to answer Hawley’s opening question confirming his job title. 

“Mr. Ellison, you’re in my courtroom now, you’re under oath, so I suggest that you answer my questions,” Hawley said. 

Committee Chair Rand Paul (R-Ky.) intervened several times, telling Hawley, “I think we’ve made a point.”

Hawley responded, “Oh I’ll ask how I want to, Senator Paul.”

At another point in the exchange, Hawley told Ellison, “It’s my hearing, pal.” The two talked over each other with Ellison retorting, “Don’t call me pal.”

“I should call you prisoner because you ought to be in jail,” Hawley shot back.

Ellison has long been a target of Republicans. He was one of the Minnesota officials subpoenaed by the Justice Department in January over allegations that state officials interfered with the federal immigration operation. The attorney general has filed several lawsuits since the immigration enforcement operation began, challenging the federal government’s actions in the state. 

He was appearing on a panel with other Minnesota officials, including House Majority Whip Tom Emmer (R-Minn.), Minnesota State House GOP Floor Leader Harry Niska, Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison and Minnesota Department of Corrections Commissioner Paul Schnell. 

A second panel at the hearing included U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services Director Joseph Edlow, Border Protection Director Rodney Scott and acting Immigration and Customs Director Todd Lyons (ICE). Paul had demanded the three immigration officials appear before the panel after the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by Border Patrol agents in Minneapolis.

Ellison had also faced heat during the Thursday morning hearing from Sen. Ron Johnson (R-Wis.) and Sen. Ashley Moody (R-Fla.).

Johnson accused the attorney general of being responsible for the two people killed by federal law enforcement in Minnesota. 

“Attorney General, you did the exact opposite and two people are dead because you encouraged them to put themselves in harm’s way,” the senator said.

Ellison denied Johnson’s accusations.

“It was a nice theatrical performance, but it was all lies,” the attorney general said.

data.title: What US taxpayers should be asking about Venezuela and foreign oil
data.description:
When President Trump sat down with oil industry executives to pitch rebuilding Venezuela’s oil sector, one comment quietly undercut the entire premise of the meeting. Exxon’s CEO described Venezuela as “uninvestable” under current conditions, pointing to weak legal protections, unstable commercial frameworks, and decades-long risks that no serious company would ignore.

That assessment should end the conversation. Instead, it points to what would almost certainly come next: even more government support for foreign oil and gas extraction layered on top of subsidies that already exist.

Ordinary American taxpayers are already carrying more than their fair share. Through a web of industry-specific tax breaks, the U.S. tax code routinely subsidizes overseas fossil fuel extraction. Research from the FACT Coalition found that U.S. oil and gas multinationals collectively pay billions more in taxes to foreign governments than to the U.S. Treasury, even though, in the aggregate, they produce more oil and gas domestically than in all other countries combined.

Between 2018 and 2024, publicly disclosed data from 11 major U.S. oil and gas companies show that less than one-fifth of their total reported tax obligations were owed to the U.S. That is lost revenue that could be funding American infrastructure, workers and communities.

This reflects an America-last policy hiding in plain sight.

Now, proposals to reimburse or guarantee investments in Venezuela would pile new public costs on top of these existing subsidies. At a time of record deficits and intense pressure on working families, asking taxpayers to financially support risky overseas oil projects is indefensible and bad for American workers. 

The domestic oil industry is already shedding jobs. Across oil-producing regions, layoffs are accelerating as prices fall, drilling slows and companies delay new projects. In places like West Texas, lower prices don’t feel like relief at the pump. They mean fewer rigs, fewer hours and fewer paychecks. Restaurants empty out. Small businesses close. Local economies stall.

Flooding the global market with additional oil would likely push prices down even further. That might please politicians chasing short-term optics, but it would deepen the pain for U.S. oil workers and the communities that depend on them. If anything, existing tax breaks may already be costing American jobs by rewarding companies for going abroad rather than investing at home.

Since 2017, U.S. oil and gas companies have owed an average federal tax rate of just 12 percent on their domestic income, which is well below the 21 percent statutory rate, thanks to a maze of subsidies and foreign tax credit rules unique to the industry. These provisions do not protect jobs. Rather, they encourage offshoring, drain public coffers and leave working families to pick up the slack.

This is the core contradiction at the heart of the Venezuela pitch. We are told subsidies are necessary to unlock new supply, yet that supply would undermine domestic workers. We are told this is about energy security, yet it increases dependence on unstable overseas production. We are told taxpayers will benefit, yet the costs are clear and the gains speculative at best.

The oil executives know this. That is why their response to the White House’s overtures was cautious, noncommittal and laced with conditions. When companies with decades of experience and billions at stake hesitate to invest their own capital, taxpayers should not be asked to step in as guarantors of last resort.

Congress has a better option — and a responsibility to act.

Rather than expand fossil fuel subsidies, lawmakers should close offshore tax loopholes that reward overseas extraction, reform how foreign profits are taxed, and require greater tax transparency so that Americans can see where profits are earned and taxes are paid. These reforms would free up revenue for priorities that actually deliver value for Americans.

The lesson from the meeting is not that Venezuela needs U.S. oil companies. It is that if a project is “uninvestable” on its own merits, it has no business being propped up by American taxpayers.

Taxpayers should not be asked to bail out oil companies in Venezuela or anywhere else. The era of quiet subsidies and America-last energy policy should end now.

Ian Gary is the executive director of the Financial Accountability and Corporate Transparency Coalition.

data.title: US troops withdraw from al-Tanf military base, Syrian army takes control
data.description:
United States troops withdrew from a key military base in southeastern Syria as the Syrian army took control, shedding the U.S. presence in the country deployed to fight against the Islamic State (ISIS). 

Through coordination between Syrian and U.S. forces, the interim government military took over the al-Tanf base and its surroundings and began deploying along the nation’s borders with Iraq and Jordan, the Syrian Ministry of Defense said in a statement. 

The U.S. Central Command (Centcom) confirmed the drawdown of troops from the base, where the U.S. has had presence since 2016, saying on Thursday it was part of a “deliberate and conditions-based transition” by Combined Joint Task Force – Operation Inherent Resolve. 

“U.S. forces remain poised to respond to any ISIS threats that arise in the region as we support partner-led efforts to prevent the terrorist network’s resurgence,” Adm. Brad Cooper, the Centcom commander, said in a statement. “Maintaining pressure on ISIS is essential to protecting the U.S. homeland and strengthening regional security.”

The Hill has reached out to Centcom for additional comment. 

The drawdown comes as Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa is looking to grasp control of the country’s territory, which has been sliced up during the nation’s civil war. Al-Sharaa’s forces led an offensive last month in northeastern Syria against Kurdish-led militias, the Syrian Democratic Forces, long supported by the U.S., garnering control of the region’s oil fields. 

Last month, President Trump’s special envoy to Syria, Tom Barrack, said that the U.S. would shift to working with Al-Sharaa’s forces on counterterrorism operations in the country. 

The number of U.S. troops in Syria has fluctuated in recent years, increasing following Hamas’s Oct. 7, 2023, attack on southern Israel to over 2,000, but the Pentagon started pulling around 600 U.S. troops last year, pointing to the success in combating ISIS. 

The U.S. still has a military presence at other bases inside Syria. 

The U.S. has gone after ISIS recently, striking over 100 targets with more than 350 precision munitions, while either capturing or killing more than 50 ISIS terrorists, according to Centcom.

data.title: Netanyahu skeptical about Trump's talks with Iran, but says 'good deal' possible
data.description:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu on Thursday said he is skeptical that a quality deal can be reached with Iran that neuters its threats to the region, but put support behind President Trump’s efforts to reach an agreement. 

Netanyahu made the remarks following a meeting with Trump at the White House on Wednesday. After that meeting, Trump said “nothing definitive” reached in his conversations with Netanyahu, and the two did not do a joint press conference.

Netanyahu on Thursday said his talks with Trump were “primarily” focused on Iran, and described a “very close, very genuine and very open relationship” with the U.S. president.

Netanyahu said he told Trump he’s skeptical of “the quality of an agreement with Iran,” but said Trump is creating “conditions for achieving a good deal.” 

“The President believes that the Iranians have already learned who they are dealing with,” Netanyahu said.

In June, Trump authorized strikes following a White House deadline for Iran to come to an agreement with the U.S.  The U.S. and Israel coordinated strikes on Iran’s air defenses, military sites and nuclear facilities.

In recent weeks, Trump has dispatched additional military assets to the Middle East, including the U.S.S. Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group, and floated sending a second strike-group, as the U.S. and Iran have resumed negotiations over its nuclear program. 

“I think that the conditions he is creating, combined with the fact that they surely understand they made a mistake last time by not reaching an agreement, could lead to the conditions for achieving a good deal,” Netanyahu said. 

“He wanted to hear my opinion. I won’t hide from you that I expressed general skepticism about the quality of any agreement with Iran,” he continued. 

“But I said that if an agreement is indeed reached, it must include elements that are very important to us, to Israel — and, in my opinion, not only to Israel.”

Israel’s red-lines are no Iranian nuclear enrichment, ending its ballistic missile program and ending Iran’s support for terrorist proxies.

Iranian officials have said they are willing to discuss their nuclear activities but claimed that uranium enrichment – the fuel for a nuclear weapon – is a national right. Iran claims its nuclear program is for peaceful purposes but has stockpiled enough enriched uranium to quickly create a bomb. 

In 2018, Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Obama-era nuclear deal with Iran, the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action, which imposed limits on Iran’s nuclear enrichment in exchange for sanctions relief.

data.title: Trump nominates hospitality executive to lead National Park Service
data.description:
President Trump is nominating a food service and hospitality executive to lead the National Park Service (NPS).

Trump’s pick to lead the U.S.’s national parks, Scott Socha, is Delaware North’s president of parks and resorts, as well as president of Delaware North Australia.

Delaware North has contracted with the NPS.

According to Delaware North’s website, its parks and resorts subsidiary provides services in seven national parks and operates lodging in five communities near parks including near Yosemite.

Socha has been with the company since 1999.

His appointment requires Senate confirmation.  

Trump also announced he would nominate Kevin Lilly, who worked in wealth management prior to reportedly joining the Trump administration, to lead the Fish and Wildlife Service.

data.title: Watch live: Trump, Zeldin expected to announce repeal of climate endangerment finding
data.description:
President Trump and Environmental Protection Agency Administrator Lee Zeldin are set to announce plans Thursday to repeal the landmark 2009 legal finding that climate change poses a threat to the public.

The move marks a significant escalation in the administration’s fight against green policies and its efforts to deny climate change. The legal finding, known as the endangerment finding, underpins the nation’s climate rules.

It also comes a day after Trump directed the Pentagon to purchase more power from coal plants in a boost for the declining fossil fuel.

The event is scheduled to begin at 1:30 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Judge blocks Hegseth, Pentagon bid to punish Mark Kelly over 'illegal orders' video
data.description:
A federal judge on Thursday blocked the Pentagon’s efforts to censure Sen. Mark Kelly and lower his retirement rank in the wake of a video in which the Arizona senator and other Democrats called on service members to reject unlawful orders.    

U.S. District Judge Richard Leon, an appointee of former President George W. Bush, noted the military principal that servicemembers enjoy weakened First Amendment protections to preserve discipline in the armed forces, but said that no court had ever extended that doctrine to retired service members.  

He said he would not be the first to do so. 

“This Court has all it needs to conclude that Defendants have trampled on Senator Kelly’s First Amendment freedoms and threatened the constitutional liberties of millions of military retirees,” the judge wrote.  

Leon quoted Bob Dylan to say, “You don’t need a weatherman to know which way the wind blows.” 

“To say the least, our retired veterans deserve more respect from their Government,” the judge added, “and our Constitution demands they receive it!”  

Kelly, a retired Navy captain, sued last month after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced the Pentagon had initiated retirement grade determination proceedings and issued a formal letter of censure over the November video.  

Five other Democrats with either intelligence or military backgrounds joined Kelly in calling on troops to “refuse illegal orders”: Sen. Elissa Slotkin (Mich.) and Reps. Chris Deluzio (Pa.), Maggie Goodlander (N.H.), Chrissy Houlahan (Pa.) and Jason Crow (Colo.). 

At a hearing last week, Kelly’s attorneys argued that the Department of Defense sought to inflict “unprecedented punishment” on the senator for his protected speech. They warned that the consequences of a ruling against Kelly could have ramifications beyond the senator.  

“Their rule would extend to every single retired service member in the country,” said lawyer Benjamin Mizer. 

The Justice Department said that the speech of veterans is more limited than that of regular civilians, contending that retirement from active service is not the same as separation from the military.  

DOJ lawyer John Bailey urged Kelly to see the Pentagon’s process through before involving the courts and warned that, without deference to the military, Leon risked undermining the armed forces and its internal processes.  

“That would send a message that the military does not have control of its own forces,” Bailey said.   

At a press conference Wednesday, Kelly called the probe “straight from the authoritarian playbook” but suggested it’s a fight that’s bigger than him alone.  

“I want to be clear about something: This is not a ‘good-news’ story,” Kelly said at a press conference Wednesday. “This is a story about how hard Donald Trump and his cronies are trying to break our system in order to silence anyone who lawfully speaks out against them. 

“And to send a signal to every American that they better think twice before they speak out, or else they might be next,” he added.  

DEVELOPING

data.title: How to raise birth rates is the wrong question: Here's what we should be asking
data.description:
As a demographer who studies American childbearing decision-making and behaviors, I’m increasingly being pulled into the near-constant discourse on how to raise birth rates, spurred by the record low total fertility rate of 1.6 births per woman the U.S. reached in 2024. 

Conservative groups, like the Heritage Foundation, have been some of the loudest voices, putting forth policies they believe will raise birth rates by encouraging heterosexual marriage and discouraging divorce, restricting access to social welfare programs and — perhaps counterintuitively — limiting access to IVF. On the other side of the spectrum, some argue that leftists and feminists should present their own progressive solutions to raise birth rates, like child care or paid leave.

Regardless of where they’re coming from, pronatalist efforts ignore a truth from demographic research: Policies to raise birth rates do not result in substantial or sustained increases.  

Declining birth rates have real implications for population size and composition, the labor market and economic growth, tax revenue and programs funded by taxes, such as Social Security, and local communities. But attempts to raise birth rates are rarely successful. That’s the conclusion of a recent review of decades of other countries’ family policies that were either explicitly designed to raise birth rates or could have done so indirectly, by reducing the costs or challenges associated with childrearing.

The review considered three specific types of policies: cash benefits (“baby bonuses,” family allowances, tax relief), parental leave and child care support. The authors conclude that although policy — primarily paid parental leave and a robust child care infrastructure — can raise birth rates, such efforts have only modest effects, raising the number of children per woman by between 0.1 and 0.2. That is hardly enough to push the U.S. total fertility rate above the well-known (if misunderstood) “replacement-level fertility” threshold of 2.1.

Efforts to raise birth rates by restricting access to abortion don’t work either. Although abortion bans can produce short-term increases in birth rates, they ultimately lower the number of children women have over their lifetime, along with increasing the risk of negative health outcomes for mothers and infants and a host of other social ills.

Asking what can be done to raise birth rates, then, is the wrong question. Demographic research consistently shows that birth rates generally move in one direction: They decline over time. Further, population targets that focus on childbearing often ignore women’s reproductive autonomy, leading to coercion and risks to women’s health.

A better question is: Can we do more to make parenthood easier for people who want to have children? Certainly, many people are unable to have as many children as they would like, and policies such as affordable, accessible child care and paid family leave might help people reach their personal family goals. Child care and paid leave also improve children’s outcomes, support parents’ mental and physical health and contribute to a stronger, more productive labor force. 

We should continue to welcome new initiatives, like the child-care expansions recently announced in New York. Similarly, expanding access to infertility treatment could help people achieve their own childbearing goals and boost birth rates slightly. But we should support these things regardless of whether they result in sizable increases in birth rates over the long term.  

A second critical question is how do we support the population that we currently, and will likely, have in the future, given current trends? Chasing economic growth by focusing on indefinite population growth, achieved primarily through more births, is a Ponzi scheme. As with other high-income countries, low birth rates are likely to be a persistent reality for the U.S., but a 2024 economic review piece notes that “a thoughtful and consistent policy response can mitigate most of the adverse consequences” of low fertility. Others have suggested that low fertility — with proper planning — can even be beneficial. 

The constant emphasis on raising birth rates deflects attention away from levers that would be more effective in addressing the broader consequences of low fertility, such as immigration policy that welcomes immigrants as an integral part of the U.S. economy. It takes discussions about how we structure and fund Social Security off the table and sidelines conversations about accommodating older workers and building infrastructure to address the health needs of an aging population.

It’s worth noting, too, that children born today would not immediately contribute to the labor market or produce tax revenue but would actually be net economic burdens for two decades.  

It’s not that low birth rates don’t present real challenges. It’s that the solution to those challenges isn’t to try to raise birth rates. We need to shift the conversation, asking ourselves how we can develop a sustainable response to demographic change by adapting and investing in the populations and institutions we have rather than chasing unattainable population targets.

Karen Benjamin Guzzo is director of the Carolina Population Center and a professor in the Department of Sociology at University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.
